title: Chocolate and beetroot cake
subtitle: A moist, earthy chocolate cake with grated raw beetroot
excerpt: Raw grated beetroot folded into a dark chocolate sponge: the vegetable disappears into the crumb during baking, leaving extra moisture and a subtle earthiness behind. Finished with a dark ganache.
sourceNotes: The chocolate and beetroot pairing in baking emerged in British food writing in the 1990s and early 2000s, partly as a response to interest in vegetables in unexpected places. Nigel Slater's Tender (2009) includes a version of this cake that established the approach of using raw (not pre-cooked) grated beetroot for its superior moisture contribution. The ganache used here follows the soft-set (1:1 chocolate to cream) method rather than a firmer truffle ratio.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze without the ganache, wrapped in cling film, for up to 2 months. Ganache after thawing.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake improves after 24 hours. Keep in an airtight tin at room temperature for up to 3 days.

baking.decoratingTechnique: dark chocolate ganache poured over the cooled cake and left to set

content.text: Use raw, uncooked beetroot grated on the coarse side of a box grater. Pre-cooked beetroot from a vacuum pack is too wet and will make the batter sodden. Wear gloves when grating: raw beetroot stains everything it touches for an hour or more.
The beetroot does not need to be squeezed dry. Its moisture is part of what makes this cake so dense and long-lasting. Do not add any extra liquid to the batter beyond what is in the recipe.

Heat the oven to 170°C fan. Grease and line a 20 cm round cake tin. Whisk the {{vegetable-oil}}, {{eggs}}, and {{vanilla-extract}} together. Stir in the {{dark-brown-sugar}}. Fold in the {{plain-flour}}, {{cocoa-powder}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{fine-sea-salt}}. Fold in the grated {{beetroot}} until evenly distributed. Spoon into the tin and bake for 35 to 40 minutes until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean. The cake will be very dark. Cool in the tin for 15 minutes, then turn out onto a rack to cool completely.

Place the {{dark-chocolate}} in a heatproof bowl. Bring the {{double-cream}} to the boil in a small saucepan and pour over the chocolate. Leave for 2 minutes, then stir from the centre outward until smooth and glossy. Leave at room temperature for 15 minutes until the ganache thickens to a pourable coating consistency. Place the cooled cake on a rack set over a tray. Pour the ganache over the top and let it flow down the sides naturally. Leave for 30 minutes to set before cutting.
